md: A **coordinate plane** is two number lines crossing at right angles. The horizontal one is the **x-axis**; the vertical one is the **y-axis**. They cross at the **origin**, which is the point $(0, 0)$.

Every point on the plane has an *address* — two numbers in parentheses, like $(3, 4)$. The first number is the **x-coordinate** (how far right or left); the second is the **y-coordinate** (how far up or down).

blocks.title: x first, y second
blocks.body: Always — alphabetical order, in the order you'd read it. The point $(3, 4)$ is *not* the same as $(4, 3)$.

md: **For $(-2, 3)$:** start at origin, move 2 left (x is negative), 3 up. **For $(1, -2)$:** move 1 right, 2 down. **For $(-3, -1)$:** move 3 left, 1 down.
Each colored point above is plotted from its coordinates. Notice the dotted lines — they trace the path you'd take from the origin: across first, then up or down.

blocks.title: Points on the axes
blocks.body: If a coordinate is $0$, the point sits on an axis. $(5, 0)$ is on the x-axis. $(0, -3)$ is on the y-axis. $(0, 0)$ is the origin itself.

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: Plot $(x, y)$ by going $x$ steps right (or left if negative), then $y$ steps up (or down if negative). x first, y second. The origin is your starting point.
